summary: Gruber responds to the fatal shooting of Renee Good by an ICE agent in Minneapolis, calling it unambiguously a murder and criticizing the Trump administration's attempts to frame it as self-defense or domestic terrorism. He references the New York Times' frame-by-frame video analysis that demolishes the official narrative. The bulk of the post is devoted to praising bystander Caitlin Callenson, whose steady, close-range iPhone footage captured the shooting and the agent's face, ultimately leading to his identification. Gruber reflects honestly on whether he'd have had the courage to keep filming after shots were fired, concluding that Callenson's decision to move closer to danger rather than flee was truly extraordinary. He urges readers to seriously imagine themselves in that moment. The post ends with a characteristically Gruber-ian kicker about Callenson shooting in widescreen.
key_points: The NYT frame-by-frame analysis makes the administration's self-defense and domestic terrorism claims transparently absurd
Gruber insists on calling the killing a murder rather than using euphemistic language, invoking Orwell's warning about rejecting the evidence of your eyes and ears
Bystander Caitlin Callenson's courage in continuing to film — and moving closer — after shots were fired is the moral center of the post
Gruber candidly admits he might not have had the same courage, reasoning that filming agents who just committed an unjustified killing makes you a logical next target
Callenson's footage directly led to the identification of the ICE agent, Jonathan Ross
money_quotes: Let's stop pussyfooting around what happened here. This ICE agent murdered Renee Good, in broad daylight, in front of many witnesses and multiple cameras.
The Party told you to reject the evidence of your eyes and ears. It was their final, most essential command.
It would make more sense to shoot someone filming the scene than it did to shoot Renee Good in the first place.
Good's killing was utterly senseless. Shooting a witness with a running camera and then destroying their phone to eliminate the evidence (and a witness) would make some sense. Sick sense, but sense.
She proceeded into the scene to get closer to Good's vehicle after it crashed into a parked car, Mr. Brown-style.
Callenson's courage in the face of obvious danger is just remarkable. My god. She rose to the moment in a crucible of chaos, insanity, and murderous violence.

We all need to think about what she did, to really imagine ourselves in the same moment — the danger she stood up to, and the principles she stood up for — if we hope to do the same if a similar moment comes to us.
And, to top it off, she had the presence of mind to shoot her historic footage in widescreen.
key_insight: When the state kills without justification and then lies about it, the courage of ordinary citizens who document the truth with their cameras becomes an essential act of democratic resistance.
